---
title: "01 · Limits — The Idea of Approach"
subtitle: "What value does $f(x)$ approach as $x$ approaches $a$?"
lesson-id: "t4.limits"
tier: 4
prerequisites: ["t3.exp-log-functions", "t3.unit-circle"]
unlocks: ["t4.continuity", "t4.derivative", "t4.definite-integral"]
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 35
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T3 L06 — Exp & Log Functions](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/06-exp-log-functions/index.qmd)
   and [T3 L08 — Unit Circle](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/08-unit-circle/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable evaluating functions at specific points, e.g. $f(2) = 5$?
3. Recall $0.\overline{9} = 1$ from [T1 L08 Stretch S2](../../tier-1-foundations/08-decimals/index.qmd)?
   That's a limit in disguise.
:::

## Touch

Pick a function and a target $x$-value $a$. The widget plots and
shows the value $f(a)$ along with the *limit* — the value $f$ approaches
as $x \to a$.

```{ojs}
viewof fnSel = Inputs.select(["f(x) = x²", "f(x) = (x²−1)/(x−1) — undefined at 1!", "f(x) = sin(x)/x — undefined at 0!", "f(x) = 1/x"], {value: "f(x) = (x²−1)/(x−1) — undefined at 1!", label: "Function:"})
viewof aTarget = Inputs.range([-3, 3], {value: 1, step: 0.1, label: "x → a:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const W = 720, H = 320, pad = 30;
  const xMin = -3, xMax = 3, yMin = -2, yMax = 5;
  const px = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);
  const py = v => H - pad - ((v - yMin) / (yMax - yMin)) * (H - 2 * pad);
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px; background:#fafafa;");
  // axes
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad).attr("y1", py(0)).attr("y2", py(0)).attr("stroke", "#1f2933");
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", px(0)).attr("x2", px(0)).attr("y1", pad).attr("y2", H - pad).attr("stroke", "#1f2933");
  // function
  let fn, undefAt = null, limitAt = null;
  if (fnSel === "f(x) = x²") { fn = x => x * x; }
  else if (fnSel.includes("(x²−1)/(x−1)")) { fn = x => Math.abs(x - 1) < 0.001 ? null : (x*x - 1) / (x - 1); undefAt = 1; limitAt = 2; }
  else if (fnSel.includes("sin(x)/x")) { fn = x => Math.abs(x) < 0.001 ? null : Math.sin(x) / x; undefAt = 0; limitAt = 1; }
  else { fn = x => Math.abs(x) < 0.001 ? null : 1 / x; }
  // plot
  let pathD = "";
  for (let x = xMin; x <= xMax; x += 0.02) {
    const y = fn(x);
    if (y === null || y < yMin - 1 || y > yMax + 1) { if (pathD) svg.append("path").attr("d", pathD).attr("fill","none").attr("stroke","#c2185b").attr("stroke-width",2); pathD = ""; continue; }
    pathD += (pathD ? " L " : "M ") + `${px(x)},${py(y)}`;
  }
  if (pathD) svg.append("path").attr("d", pathD).attr("fill","none").attr("stroke","#c2185b").attr("stroke-width",2);
  // hole at undefined point
  if (undefAt !== null && limitAt !== null) {
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", px(undefAt)).attr("cy", py(limitAt)).attr("r", 5).attr("fill","#fff").attr("stroke","#c2185b").attr("stroke-width",2);
  }
  // marker for x = aTarget
  const yAtA = fn(aTarget);
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", px(aTarget)).attr("x2", px(aTarget)).attr("y1", pad).attr("y2", H - pad).attr("stroke","#1565c0").attr("stroke-dasharray","4 4");
  if (yAtA !== null && yAtA >= yMin && yAtA <= yMax) {
    svg.append("circle").attr("cx", px(aTarget)).attr("cy", py(yAtA)).attr("r", 6).attr("fill","#1565c0");
    svg.append("text").attr("x", px(aTarget) + 8).attr("y", py(yAtA) - 8).attr("font-size","12").attr("fill","#1565c0").attr("font-weight","bold").text(`(${aTarget.toFixed(2)}, ${yAtA.toFixed(3)})`);
  } else {
    svg.append("text").attr("x", px(aTarget) + 8).attr("y", py(0) - 8).attr("font-size","12").attr("fill","#1565c0").attr("font-weight","bold").text("undefined here");
  }
  return svg.node();
}
```

A **limit** asks: as $x$ gets arbitrarily close to $a$, what value
does $f(x)$ approach? The answer can exist even when $f(a)$ itself
doesn't.

## Wonder

1. Pick "$f(x) = (x^2-1)/(x-1)$ — undefined at 1." Move $a$ near 1.
   What does $f(x)$ approach?
2. Try "$f(x) = \sin(x)/x$ — undefined at 0." What value does it
   approach as $x \to 0$?
3. Pick "$f(x) = 1/x$." What happens as $x \to 0$?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Compute (just by substitution) the limits:

(a) $\lim_{x \to 2} (3x + 1)$
(b) $\lim_{x \to 0} \cos(x)$
(c) $\lim_{x \to 1} (x^2 + 2x - 3)$

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
(a) $3 \cdot 2 + 1 = 7$.
(b) $\cos 0 = 1$.
(c) $1 + 2 - 3 = 0$.

For *continuous* functions (Lesson 02), the limit equals the value:
$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$. Most everyday limits are this easy.
</details>

### Problem 2

Compute $\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1}$.

(*Direct substitution* gives $0/0$, which is **indeterminate** —
neither defined nor "infinity". Try simplifying first.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Factor the numerator: $x^2 - 1 = (x - 1)(x + 1)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1} = \dfrac{(x-1)(x+1)}{x - 1} = x + 1$ (for $x \neq 1$).

So $\lim_{x \to 1} \dfrac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1} = \lim_{x \to 1} (x + 1) = 2$.

The function isn't *defined* at $x = 1$ (denominator zero), but its
*limit* as $x \to 1$ is **2**. The graph has a "hole" at $(1, 2)$;
the function gets arbitrarily close to 2 from both sides.

The widget shows this hole as a hollow circle.
</details>

### Problem 3

Compute $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x}$. Try plugging in small
values: $x = 0.1, 0.01, 0.001$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $\sin(0.1)/0.1 \approx 0.99833$
- $\sin(0.01)/0.01 \approx 0.99998$
- $\sin(0.001)/0.001 \approx 0.99999983$

The limit is **1**.

This is one of the most important limits in calculus. It's not
provable by simple algebra — it requires a geometric squeeze argument
(unit circle areas) or the Taylor series of $\sin$ (Tier 4+). It
underlies the derivative of $\sin x$ (Lesson 5).
</details>

### Problem 4

Compute $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{x}$ and $\lim_{x \to 0^+}
\dfrac{1}{x}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For the first: as $x$ grows huge, $1/x$ shrinks. For the second:
as $x$ approaches 0 from the *right* (positive side), $1/x$ grows
huge.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\lim_{x \to \infty} 1/x = 0$.
$\lim_{x \to 0^+} 1/x = +\infty$ (and $\lim_{x \to 0^-} 1/x = -\infty$).

**Limits at infinity** ($x \to \infty$) describe long-run behaviour.
**One-sided limits** ($x \to a^+$ or $a^-$) consider approach from
just one side. **Infinite limits** describe blow-ups.

The two-sided limit $\lim_{x \to 0} 1/x$ does **not exist** because
left and right disagree.
</details>

### Problem 5

A limit that *should* be 0 but is *actually* 1: compute $\lim_{x \to
0^+} x \cdot \ln x$.

(Hint: $\ln x \to -\infty$ as $x \to 0^+$, but $x \to 0$. What
happens to the product?)

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try numerical: $x = 0.1, 0.01, 0.001$. Compute $x \ln x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $0.1 \cdot \ln(0.1) \approx -0.230$
- $0.01 \cdot \ln(0.01) \approx -0.046$
- $0.001 \cdot \ln(0.001) \approx -0.0069$

The limit is **0**: the $x$ factor crushes the $\ln x$ infinity. This
is an "$0 \cdot \infty$" *indeterminate form* — its value depends on
which factor wins.

Resolving such forms rigorously needs **L'Hôpital's rule** (Tier 4
later) or careful estimation. The fact that limits resolve such
ambiguities is exactly what makes them useful.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **limit** of $f(x)$ as $x$ approaches $a$ is the value $L$ that
$f(x)$ gets arbitrarily close to as $x$ gets arbitrarily close to $a$
(but $x \neq a$):
$$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = L.$$

**One-sided limits:**

- $\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$ — from the right.
- $\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x)$ — from the left.

The two-sided limit exists *iff* both one-sided limits exist and are
equal.

**Limits at infinity:** $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = L$ if $f(x)$ gets
close to $L$ as $x$ grows without bound.

**Indeterminate forms:** $\dfrac{0}{0}, \dfrac{\infty}{\infty}, 0 \cdot
\infty, \infty - \infty, 0^0, 1^\infty, \infty^0$. These require *more
work* (factoring, conjugates, L'Hôpital).

**Limit laws** (when the individual limits exist):

- $\lim (f + g) = \lim f + \lim g$.
- $\lim (f \cdot g) = \lim f \cdot \lim g$.
- $\lim (f/g) = \lim f / \lim g$ (if $\lim g \neq 0$).
- $\lim c \cdot f = c \cdot \lim f$ for constant $c$.

**Famous limits:**

- $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{\sin x}{x} = 1$.
- $\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{n}\right)^n = e$.

:::

> *Limit asks where the function is heading, not where it lands;*
> *Two-sided limits need agreement of both one-sided limits;*
> *Indeterminate forms (0/0, ∞/∞, ...) need work; not the value, but the journey.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Compute $\lim_{x \to 4} \dfrac{x^2 - 16}{x - 4}$.

A competent solver:

1. **Direct sub:** $\dfrac{0}{0}$ — indeterminate.
2. **Factor:** $x^2 - 16 = (x - 4)(x + 4)$.
3. **Cancel:** $\dfrac{(x-4)(x+4)}{x - 4} = x + 4$ for $x \neq 4$.
4. **Substitute now:** $\lim_{x \to 4} (x + 4) = 8$.

**Answer: 8.** This pattern — substitute, get 0/0, factor, cancel,
substitute again — is the standard playbook for rational limits.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $\lim_{x \to 3} (2x - 5)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2 \cdot 3 - 5 = 1$.
</details>

**W2.** $\lim_{x \to 0} (3x^2 + 2)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
2.
</details>

**W3.** $\lim_{x \to \pi} \cos(x)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos \pi = -1$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** $\lim_{x \to 2} \dfrac{x^2 - 4}{x - 2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Factor: $(x-2)(x+2)/(x-2) = x + 2$. Limit: $4$.
</details>

**P2.** $\lim_{x \to \infty} \dfrac{2x^2 + 3}{x^2 + 5}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Divide top and bottom by $x^2$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{2 + 3/x^2}{1 + 5/x^2} \to \dfrac{2 + 0}{1 + 0} = 2$.

For rational functions at infinity: ratio of leading coefficients (if
degrees match).
</details>

**P3.** $\lim_{x \to 0} \dfrac{1 - \cos x}{x}$. (Hint: multiply top
and bottom by $1 + \cos x$.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{(1 - \cos x)(1 + \cos x)}{x(1 + \cos x)} = \dfrac{1 - \cos^2 x}{x(1 + \cos x)} = \dfrac{\sin^2 x}{x(1 + \cos x)} = \dfrac{\sin x}{x} \cdot \dfrac{\sin x}{1 + \cos x}$.

As $x \to 0$: first factor $\to 1$, second $\to 0/(1+1) = 0$. So
limit is **0**.

Conjugate-multiplication is a powerful trick for limits involving
$1 - \cos$ or square roots.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Compute $\lim_{n \to \infty} (1 + 1/n)^n$ numerically for $n =
1, 10, 100, 1000, 10000$. What number does it approach?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
- $n = 1$: $(1 + 1)^1 = 2$
- $n = 10$: $(1.1)^{10} \approx 2.594$
- $n = 100$: $\approx 2.704$
- $n = 1000$: $\approx 2.717$
- $n = 10000$: $\approx 2.71815$

The limit is $e \approx 2.71828\ldots$ — Euler's number.

This is the **classic definition** of $e$ — the base of natural
exponentials. Compound interest "continuously compounded" gives
exactly this growth factor.
</details>

**S2.** **Why limits matter for ML.** A neural network is "trained" by
many tiny updates: $w_{n+1} = w_n - \eta \nabla L(w_n)$. As $n \to
\infty$ (and $\eta$ → 0 carefully), the discrete updates approach a
continuous *gradient flow*. Why is this useful?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Continuous-time analysis** (using calculus, Lessons 03+) is often
more tractable than the discrete recursion. Researchers prove
*convergence theorems* about gradient descent by studying the limiting
ODE $\dot{w}(t) = -\nabla L(w(t))$ — a *differential equation* (Lesson
12). Conclusions then transfer back to the actual training algorithm.

Limits — discrete becoming continuous — are the *hidden engine* of
nearly every theoretical result in optimisation and ML.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\lim_{x \to a} f(x) = f(a)$ always."** Only when $f$ is
   *continuous* at $a$ (Lesson 02). Limits exist for many functions
   that aren't even defined at $a$.
2. **"$0/0 = 0$."** **Undefined.** $0/0$ is an indeterminate form;
   it can equal *anything* depending on which $0$s shrink fastest.
3. **"$\infty$ is a number."** No — it's a description of unbounded
   behaviour. "$\lim = \infty$" means "grows without bound", not "equals
   the number $\infty$".
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain why $\lim_{x \to 1} (x^2 - 1)/(x - 1) = 2$ even
   though the function isn't defined at $x = 1$?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute $\lim_{x \to 0} (3x + 5)$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: 5.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[02 · Continuity](../02-continuity/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **P3** (conjugate trick for trig limits).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T3 L06 — Exp & Log Functions](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/06-exp-log-functions/index.qmd)**
- **[T3 L08 — Unit Circle](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/08-unit-circle/index.qmd)** —
  for $\sin x / x$ and other trig limits.

### What this unlocks

- **[02 · Continuity](../02-continuity/index.qmd)** — defined via limits.
- **[03 · Derivative](../03-derivative/index.qmd)** — defined as the
  limit of a slope.
- **[08 · Definite Integral](../08-definite-integral/index.qmd)** —
  defined as a limit of Riemann sums.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Continuous-time analysis (S2) of gradient
descent and other optimisation algorithms; convergence guarantees;
all use limits.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Limits are the gateway to **real
analysis** (Tier 5). The careful $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition is
the formal version of the intuitive "approach" idea here.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Physics & engineering.** Instantaneous speed (limit of average
speed), instantaneous power (limit of energy/time), instantaneous
flow rate — all *limits of ratios* in the world.
:::
